edge: Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) is known for its capability in photo-realistic 3D reconstruction
Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) is also known for its impressive capability in novel view synthesis
The paper introduces a novel bundle adjusted deblur Neural Radiance Fields (BAD-NeRF) as a robust solution to motion blurred images
Image degradation, such as image motion blur in low-light conditions, can further affect the rendering quality of Neural Radiance Fields
BAD-NeRF recovers the camera motion trajectories during exposure time to improve the quality of the final image
Acquiring accurate 3D scene geometry and appearance from a set of 2D images is a long-standing problem in computer vision
Accurate 3D scene geometry is essential for applications like novel view image synthesis
Accurate 3D scene geometry is also crucial for robotic navigation applications
Robotics navigation involves interacting with the 3D scene for effective movement and decision making.
Classic approaches represent the 3D scene explicitly using 3D point cloud.
Classic approaches represent the 3D scene explicitly using triangular mesh.
Classic approaches represent the 3D scene explicitly using volumetric grid.
Recent advancements in implicit 3D representation include the usage of Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) for photo-realistic 3D reconstruction.
NeRF takes a 5D vector as input for spatial location and viewing direction of the sampled 3D point.
NeRF enables novel view image synthesis using well posed multi-view images.
NeRF has enabled photo-realistic 3D reconstruction through deep neural networks.
NeRF computes pixel intensity or depth through differentiable volume rendering.
Limited work has been proposed to address the issue of training NeRF with motion blurred images.
Motion blurred images pose challenges to existing NeRF training pipeline.
Images are considered sharp when they are in focus and have clear details.
Blurred images are those that lack sharpness and clear definition of details.
Accurate camera poses are usually required to train NeRF for better performance.
It is difficult to obtain accurate camera poses from blurred images due to the lack of clear information in the images.
COLMAP is used to recover accurate poses from a set of motion blurred images.
Addressing challenges like obtaining accurate poses and training with motion blurred images can enhance NeRF's performance.
Integrating the real physical image formation process of a motion blurred image into NeRF training can improve performance.
A linear motion model in SE(3) space is used to represent camera motion trajectory within exposure time.

Following the real physical image formation model can help in synthesizing blurry images.

The formation model uses NeRF to estimate both camera motion trajectories and virtual sharp images, contributing to the synthesis of motion blurred images.

The modified model, BAD-NeRF, is derived from NeRF by incorporating bundle adjusted deblur techniques for image synthesis.
BAD-NeRF is evaluated using both synthetic and real datasets to demonstrate superior performance in comparison to prior works.
BAD-NeRF outperforms prior state of the art works in terms of its ability to model the image formation process of motion blurred images.
The approach based on BAD-NeRF formulation can be used to acquire high quality 3D scene representations from motion blurred images.
BAD-NeRF is able to deblur severe motion blurred images and synthesize high quality novel view images as shown through experimental validation.

BAD-NeRF learns both the 3D scene representation and recovers camera motion trajectories from motion blurred images
Motion blurred images are used to synthesize blurry images from NeRF by maximizing photo-metric consistency.
Both NeRF and the motion trajectories are estimated by maximizing the photo-metric consistency between the synthesized blurry images and the real blurry images.
NeRF is used to represent the 3D scene implicitly using Multi-layer Perceptrons (MLP) as per the general architecture of NeRF.
The 3D scene is represented implicitly using two Multi-layer Perceptrons in the architecture of NeRF.
The pixel intensity I(x) at a pixel location x is queried for a particular image with a pose Tw by shooting a ray into the 3D space.
The pixel intensity is computed by volume rendering along the ray defined by the camera center and the corresponding pixel location.
Given a particular 3D point Xw with depth λ along the ray, the pixel intensity can be computed for that point.
The camera intrinsic parameters K are assumed based on a simple pinhole camera model for ray direction and camera pose.
NeRF is queried for the corresponding view-dependent color c and volume density σ for a particular 3D point Xw.
NeRF uses Multi-layer Perceptrons parameterized with learnable parameters θ to get the color c and volume density σ for a given 3D point Xw.
The learnable parameters θ are related to Rw, potentially indicating a relationship in the image rendering process.
The viewing direction dc is transformed by the rotation matrix c from camera coordinate frame to world coordinate frame.
The predicted color ci and volume density σi are calculated for the sampled 3D points along the ray.
The distance δi and transmittance factor Ti are used to represent the probability that the ray does not hit any particle until the ith sampled point.
The predicted volume density σk and distance between neighboring points σk are related when calculating the density for each point.
The rendered pixel intensity I(x) is a function of the MLPs with learnable parameters θ, indicating a dependency in the image rendering process.
The rendered pixel intensity I(x) is affected by the camera pose Tw, suggesting a connection between the image rendering and camera position.
The bundle adjustment formulation with motion blurred images involves adjusting parameters for image rendering in the presence of motion blur.
The image formation process involves a digital camera collecting photons and converting them into electric charges during exposure.
The image formation process results in the captured image B(x), which represents the integration of virtual sharp images.

The sharp image captured at timestamp t within the exposure time is denoted as It(x), while the blurred image caused by camera motion during the exposure time is represented by B(x). Different virtual images It(x) for each timestamp t contribute to the formation of the blurred image.
The blurred image B(x) is formed due to camera motion during the exposure time, resulting in a blurry representation of the scene in contrast to the sharp images captured at each timestamp.

The pipeline of BAD-NeRF involves training NeRF to learn the 3D scene representation from a set of motion blurred images. The model deviates from traditional methods by considering the motion trajectory of each image with poses at the start and end of the exposure time, allowing for linear interpolation of intermediate virtual camera poses in SE3 space.

The photo-metric loss is utilized for jointly estimating the entire network and both the start and end poses involved in the synthesis of virtual sharp images within the exposure time. By minimizing this loss, the network can accurately generate the blurry image that reflects the real physical image formation process of a motion blurred scene.
